Figma’s IPO Plan

FIG filed to raise investment from the sale of its Class A common stock.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares, at least as indicated in the initial prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B shareholders, the co-founders, will have 15 votes per share and Dylan Field will control the company's voting power immediately after the IPO.

Class C shareholders will not have any voting rights.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be the standard 180-day period after the effective date of the prospectus, possibly subject to a hurdle %age, so smaller shareholders may be able to sell before then.

According to Figma’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use a portion of the net proceeds we receive from this offering to repay [an as-yet undisclosed amount] of outstanding indebtedness under the Revolving Credit Facility, which we intend to borrow in order to pay our anticipated tax withholding and remittance obligations related to the RSU Net Settlement.

We intend to use the remaining net proceeds, if any, from this offering for working capital and other general corporate purposes, which may include product development, general and administrative matters, and capital expenditures.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, the company said it is not currently the subject of any legal proceedings that are 'material.'

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, Allen & Company, JPMorgan, BofA Securities, Wells Fargo Securities, RBC Capital Markets, William Blair and Wolfe | Nomura Alliance.

Figma Is Growing Quickly And Producing Operating Profit

Figma seeks public investment to pay down debt and for its growth plans.

FIG’s financials have produced strong topline revenue growth, growing gross profit and variable gross margin and a swing to operating profit and cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, was $46 million.

Sales and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have varied as revenue has grown and its Sales and Marketing efficiency multiple rose 1.0x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay zero dividends and will retain any future earnings to reinvest in its growth initiatives and working capital needs.

FIG’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent lightly on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The company’s Rule of 40 results have been excellent, with strong topline revenue growth combined with solid operating margin.

The firm’s growth strategy features investing heavily in AI technologies, expanding customer use cases, evolving from a design tool set to a product development suite and further international expansion.

The market opportunity for providing graphic design software is large and expected to grow substantially in the coming years, so Figma has a large opportunity.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘emerging growth company’ status, which enables management to produce less information for shareholders, its voting control by the founder and the potential for ultra-low-cost AI technologies to disrupt from below.

However, FIG is growing rapidly and profitably, is well capitalized and positioned to perform well as it integrates new AI technologies into its offerings.

The IPO will likely be in high demand from institutional investors, so valuation discipline will be important.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: The Prognosis For PubMatic
data.description:
Shares of supply-side digital ad platform PubMatic, Inc. (NASDAQ:PUBM) have rebounded some 70% off their all-time low of $7.46 set on April 7, 2025, which represented a 90% decline from their all-time high. The ever-evolving digital advertising landscape seems to be favoring a more transparent direct supply path optimization, moving away from the supply-side model once featured by PubMatic. With the company adjusting on the fly but slated to lose money on a non-GAAP basis in FY25 and FY26 after four years in the black, this busted IPO merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

PubMatic, Inc. is a Redwood City, California-based digital advertising platform that enables advertisers to reach relevant audiences and publishers to maximize yield on their content. The company executes across all digital channels, with a focus on programmatic and AI-driven solutions, buttressed by over 900 active publishers and 50,000 advertisers (representing over 20 verticals) on its platform. PubMatic was founded in 2006 and went public in 2020, raising net proceeds of $45.0 million at $20 per share. Its stock trades just over $12.50 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $615 million.

The company is capitalized by two classes of stock. The 40.3 million shares of publicly traded Class A stock confer economic interest and one vote per share. The 8.3 million shares of privately held Class B stock bestow economic interest, ten votes per share, and convertibility into Class A stock. Under this arrangement, directors, officers, and their relevant affiliates control 68% of the voting power.

Digital Advertising Model

The digital advertising marketplace is an evolving and complex landscape consisting of brands, advertising agencies, and demand side platforms (DSPs) that represent them (a.k.a., buyers), who purchase advertising inventory from digital content creators, app developers, and supply (or selling) side platforms (SSPs) that represent them (a.k.a., publishers). PubMatic is an SSP that primarily generates revenue by charging publishers, app developers, and aggregators of smaller publishers a fee that is typically a percentage of the value of the advertising impressions monetized on its platform. In a typical waterfall model, publishers would rank SSPs according to revenue yield from selling ad inventory, with the latter receiving bid requests according to the order in which they were ranked. That model has drawbacks, making it difficult for a DSP to ascertain both who it is buying from and the efficiency of its ad spend. As such, it is giving way to supply path optimization (SPO), which had its origins in header bidding services that allow publishers to offer their ad inventory to multiple advertisers simultaneously and has rapidly evolved from there.

In response to these shifts, PubMatic has both a header bidding service (OpenWrap) and a more advanced SPO service for media buyers dubbed Activate that allows them to transparently execute direct deals with publisher inventory. It enables direct insertion order buys – essentially formal contracts between publishers and advertisers with no intermediaries – to shift to programmatic, where algorithms and AI match up buyers and sellers instantaneously. Another buyer service is Connect, which generates data and insights. The company’s Convert offering provides a unified technology platform for commerce media networks.

Digital Advertising Marketplace

Global digital advertising spend was ~$603 billion in 2023 and is expected to grow at a 10% CAGR to $993 billion in 2028. The chief purveyors are Google (GOOG, GOOGL), Meta Platforms' (FB) Facebook, and other social media companies. Within that larger universe, PubMatic’s total addressable market (TAM) is ~$122 billion, consisting of mobile app ($52 billion), digital video ($51 billion), and connected TV (CTV)/over-the-top TV ($19 billion) ad spends. The company has targeted omnichannel video (including CTV) as its niche–which currently accounts for more than 40% of its revenue—partly because the ad spend in this subspace (specifically CTV) is expected to grow at a faster pace than the balance of the market. As an SSP, PubMatic’s biggest direct competitors are Magnite (MGNI), FreeWheel Media, the SSP segment of Nexxen (NEXN), as well as divisions of mega-purveyors.

Keep in mind that as a broker in its ~$122 billion TAM, the company is actually competing for ~$7 billion in revenue, of which it has a ~4% market share. By contrast, Google dominates with a ~60% share.

Share Price Performance

Within three months of their IPO, shares of PUBM peaked at an all-time high of $76.96, buoyed by a strong market, perceptions of significant secular tailwinds for programmatic advertising, as well as the company being a beneficiary of a migration away from cookies in the digital ad world. PubMatic’s top line soared 55% to $226.9 million in its first full year as a publicly traded company (FY21). Since that time, the company has been subject to a slowdown in the overall digital advertising marketplace, with its top line growing only 12%, 4%, and 9% during FY22-FY24. Furthermore, the viability of the SSP model has come into question as it is seen as a less efficient platform for matching buyers and sellers with the advent of SPO, which permits access to curated audiences. DSPs like The Trade Desk (TTD) are circumventing gatekeeping SSPs by forging direct relationships with publishers with its OpenPath offering. That said, PubMatic has responded with its SPO Activate. Additionally, CTV, with its relatively small concentration of publishers and steady influx of new viewers from linear TV, has become a meaningful growth area for the company, which boasts 24 of the top 30 streaming publishers as clients. It now accounts for ~20% of its top line.

With internal market dynamics hurting PubMatic’s approach against a multi-year backdrop of weaker-than-expected digital ad spending, its non-GAAP earnings have fallen from $1.14 a share in FY21 to $0.57 in FY23. Even with a rebound to $0.78 a share in FY24, shares of PUBM have been on a protracted decline, bottoming out at an all-time low of $7.46 on April 7, 2025, down 90% from their all-time high.

Q1'25 Financials & Outlook

That said, the company’s stock has seen a 57% rebound off that low, somewhat aided by its Q1'25 financial report of May 8, 2025, when it posted a loss of $0.04 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $8.5 million on revenue of $63.8 million, versus a gain of $0.09 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $15.1 million on revenue of $66.7 million in Q1'24, representing declines of 144%, 44%, and 4%, respectively. Although not encouraging from an absolute standpoint, the bottom line was $0.03 better than expectations, with the top line beating by $1.8 million. PubMatic comps have been negatively impacted by one DSP buyer that changed its auction methodology in mid-2024, which was the major factor in the bad comparisons. Factoring out that buyer and political advertising in Q1'24, the company’s top line was up 21% year-over-year.

Management was optimistic in its sell-side approach, highlighting its investments in Activate, Convert, and Connect that were bearing fruit in an evolving ad spend environment, with SPO representing more than 55% of total platform activity. Furthermore, CTV revenue grew 50% year-over-year. PubMatic also boasted that its ad impressions processed in the prior twelve months were 60% greater than two years prior (TTM Q1'23), accomplished with only a 16% increase in cost of revenue over the same period. It was also cited as a positive verdict in the Google AdTech antitrust case, where it was adjudicated that the search engine giant maintained monopolies in publisher ad servers and ad exchanges, using its market power to effectively squeeze out competition. This decision should provide PubMatic with a more level playing field in the open internet.

For Q2'25, management forecasted Adj. EBITDA of $10.5 million on revenue of $68 million, based on range midpoints. It also stated that with it approaching the anniversary of the loss of business from the DSP, it was anticipating revenue growth acceleration in H2'25.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Despite the headwinds, PubMatic generated cash flow from operations of $15.6 million, en route to what will be its 12th consecutive positive year for that statistic. Its debt-free balance sheet reflected cash and marketable securities of $144.1 million on March 31, 2025. The company returns capital to shareholders via repurchases, having bought back $138 million of stock since February 2023, representing 8.7 million shares, or 17% of the then total outstanding. The board just added $100 million to its authorization through 2026, putting the amount remaining at $136.8 million.

The analyst community is mixed on the shares right now. Since first quarter results hit the wires, three analyst firms, including KeyBanc and Jefferies, maintained Hold ratings on the equity. Evercore ISI ($16 price target), Citizens JMP ($16 price target, down from $18 previously), RBC Capital ($14 price target), and Lake Street ($19 price target) reissued Buy ratings.

On average, after gains at the company’s bottom line in its first four full years as a publicly traded concern, sell-side analysts expect it to make $0.41 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $293.6 million in FY25, followed by a profit of $0.68 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $322.9 million in FY26.

Verdict:

Despite the recent rebound, with an EV/TTM Adj. EBITDA of under six, PubMatic would appear to have more room to run. And if an investor can look past its recent loss of business from a DSP and remove political advertising, the company is actually growing. However, that progress is off a lower base because of those impacts. And in FY23, investors were asked to ignore the impact of a sharp drop in business from Yahoo!

After the recent rally, the stock now trades for over 30 times forward earnings. If shares of PUBM were to pull back in the high single digits, they are worthy of investment. For now, I am staying on the sidelines.

data.title: UniFirst: End-Market Weakness Is Transitional, Initiate With Buy Rating
data.description:
UniFirst Corporation (NYSE:UNF) offers personalized workplace uniforms and protective work clothing rental services to customers, primarily serving customers in the U.S. market. Cintas Corporation (CTAS) attempted to acquire UniFirst for $275 per share in January 2025, but terminated the discussion in March 2025. I think UniFirst’s management is making efforts to accelerate its core laundry business. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $209 per share.

As illustrated in the pie chart below, the uniforms and protective work clothing rental service market is highly fragmented, with the top three players only representing 26% of the total market share. As the third-largest player, UniFirst services various end-markets, including automotive, retailers, manufacturers, restaurants, transportation, warehousing and healthcare providers. I favor its diversified end-market exposures, with no account contributing more than 10% of total revenue.

As shown in the table below, UniFirst’s core laundry business experienced sluggish growth during the global pandemic, followed by a strong recovery in the post-pandemic era. Many facilities were closed during the COVID period, which materially impacted UniFirst’s business.

One of UniFirst’s key advantages is its network of 270 customer service and distribution facilities, primarily located in the U.S. market, as shown on the map below. The massive network enables the company to serve large customers, and increase route density for the service team, which could improve its service margin and sales productivity. Having said that, UniFirst is still a small player compared to Cintas, which has 467 operational facilities and 12 distribution centers, according to its 10-K. It is quite important for UniFirst to expand its distribution center as well as local delivery routes, in my view.

UniFirst reported its Q3 FY25 result on July 2, posting 1.1% organic growth in its core laundry business. As shown in the chart below, the core laundry business has experienced very low growth in recent quarters, due to the weakness in the overall manufacturing end-market.

I think the question is when UniFirst will re-accelerate its business, which could significantly impact its valuation and stock multiples. The current weak growth in the core laundry business is caused by softness in hiring markets, especially within some traditional manufacturing sectors. For instance, domestic auto production has been quite weak since 2023, due to weak consumption and high interest rates, as depicted in the chart below. Low auto production will trigger low demands for uniforms, which will adversely affect UniFirst’s growth.

During the earnings call, the management noted that some manufacturing sectors remain very cautious about tariffs and domestic consumption. As a result, the overall industrial market is not expanding its workforce. As illustrated in the chart below, the number of manufacturing jobs has not grown in recent months. It is evident that the high interest rate and tariff uncertainty are the main reasons behind the weak manufacturing market, in my view.

Despite these headwinds, I anticipate the Fed will start to cut rates in the coming months, which could provide some support for the industrial job market. In addition, as the Trump administration has been negotiating trade deals with various countries, I anticipate the administration will sign some trade frameworks with some countries, which could ease concerns for the industrial and manufacturing market. Overall, I believe the weakness in UniFirst’s growth is temporary, and the end markets will gradually recover in the near future.

In terms of capital allocation, UniFirst has returned more than $200 million via dividends and stock buyback over the past five years, a quite generous shareholder return considering its $405 million of total free cash flow in the same period. The company has zero debt and $211.9 million in cash as of May 31, 2025. Indeed, the company has a robust balance sheet and an excellent capital allocation policy.

UniFirst maintained its full-year revenue guidance of $2.422 billion - $2.432 billion but raised its EPS guidance to $7.60 to $8.00 for FY25, as it reduced key initiative costs from a prior estimate of $12 million to $7.5 million.

Due to the current weak industrial market, I anticipate the company’s revenue will grow by 2% in FY25. During the call, the management noted that the pricing environment was quite challenging due to sluggish demands. As such, I don’t anticipate any pricing growth in FY25, but I project a 2% growth in volumes. From FY26 onwards, I forecast the company will recover to a normalized revenue growth of 4%, reflecting 2% pricing growth and 2% volume growth. It’s worth noting that prior to the global pandemic, the company delivered more than 5% growth in its core laundry business. As such, I think a 4% growth assumption is quite conservative.

I anticipate a 20bps annual margin expansion, driven by 10bps from pricing and 10bps from savings from its ERP implementation. During the call, the management attributed its reduction in key initiative cost budgets to the faster-than-expected ERP implementation. I calculate the total operating expenses will grow by 3.77% annually, leading to 20bps annual margin expansion.

With these assumptions, the discounted cash flow, or DCF, can be summarized as follows:

I calculate FCFF as follows:

WACC is calculated to be 9.6%, assuming risk-free 4.2%; beta 0.9; equity risk premium 6%; debt $0; equity $3.5 bn; tax rate 23%. I project a 4% terminal growth rate assuming 2% pricing growth and 2% volume growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adding net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $209 per share.

Key Risks

Cintas is the primary competitor of UniFirst in the U.S. market. As shown in the chart below, Cintas has sustained strong growth in recent quarters, despite the high interest rates and tariff uncertainties. It is clear that as a larger company, Cintas is less sensitive to the overall industrial end-market, and has more pricing power compared to small players like UniFirst.

UniFirst has been trying to increase its direct sales mix to penetrate more industries. The revenue contribution from direct sales could cause growth fluctuations on a quarterly basis. In addition, the company might seek a balanced approach between revenue growth and cost controls.

Lastly, during the call, the management disclosed they have not experienced significant headwinds from the current tariff; however, some vendors have started to raise prices to offset their sourcing costs. According to its 10-K, UniFirst manufactured 65% of all garments in Mexico and Nicaragua. UniFirst will face higher input costs if these countries cannot secure trade deals with the U.S. government.

Conclusion

I think UniFirst’s core laundry business will gradually recover in the near future when the Fed starts to cut interest rates and the Trump administration settles trade policies with foreign countries. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $209 per share.

data.title: SPYT: A Consistent 20% Yield? It Depends
data.description:
The Defiance S&P 500 Target Income ETF (NYSEARCA:SPYT) is a relatively new income fund based on the S&P 500 (SPY), which has flown somewhat under the radar. Its AUM has grown, but at $109M, it is relatively small.

SPYT does not offer anything particularly new or innovative in its strategy, but what sets it apart is its set objective and yield.

Aim to achieve a target annual income of 20% in the S&P 500 using options, paid out monthly.

That's a commitment to a juicy yield. As an income investor, I like - even need - to know what to expect from my investment. Most of the other income ETFs promise yields that are high, compelling or attractive, but don't say how much they aim to distribute.

There is a major caveat, however. The 20% yield may stay steady for SPYT, but could steadily drop from your perspective.

A Closer Look at SPYT

At its core, SPYT is a simple fund that is almost entirely invested in the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). This gives it exposure to the S&P 500 at a low cost, as IVV's expense ratio is only 0.03%.

SPYT's active management and 0.87% expense ratio are related to the options overlay strategy, which is used to boost income. This is by now a common feature of income ETFs, but they all have slight variations. SPYT's niche is the use of 0DTE options and its focus on at-the-money strikes.

By focusing on short-term options, we aim to offer an “enhanced” yield compared to traditional option-based strategies.

In theory, collecting premiums in each session should produce a decent yield, but it is not a straightforward task to track the exact income. More on this later.

Further information on the options strategy can be found in the prospectus.

Each day, the Fund will sell credit call spreads on the Index, focusing on options with near-term expiration. This involves selling call options at a strike price at or near the money and buying call options above that strike price. If the Index’s value rises above the upper strike price (the call spreads cap), the Fund will profit from further upside appreciation in the value of the Index.

These are the trades listed for Wednesday, 2nd July.

The lower two options were the active trades for Wednesday, 2nd July. SPYT sold the 6198 call at-the-money as the close on Tuesday, 1st July was 6198. They also bought the 6210 call.

The upper two options are the trades for the next day, Thursday, 3rd July. Again, the first call was sold and had a strike at the price of the previous day's close, and the second was bought and had a strike around 12 points above. We can see the premiums collected and paid, and calculate SPYT earned $5.73 for this call spread.

Unfortunately, this strategy means the fund will not make any gains until the upper strike price is hit. This is usually around 0.2%. As the prospectus states:

The Fund will not participate directly in approximately the first 0%-0.50% of any daily gains in the Index (or the Index ETFs). Instead, the Fund will seek to generate income (through options premiums) in lieu of market participation.

0.2% may not seem like much, but it adds up, especially when the S&P 500 is in a strong period like it is now. The premium collected does not compensate for the non-participation.

The Dividend

The potential for a 20% yield is SPYT's main attraction. It is not guaranteed and comes with the usual disclaimers:

There is no guarantee that the Fund’s investment strategy will be properly implemented, and an investor may lose some or all of its investment. While the ETF seeks to provide monthly income, there is no assurance a distribution will be made. If distributions are made, it is likely such distributions will vary greatly from one distribution to the next.

Thankfully, return of capital (ROC) comes to the rescue, as it does with many of these income funds. It doesn't really matter what the income is, SPYT can keep the distributions steady by varying the ROC, and every one of its 17 monthly payouts has been consistently near $0.30.

You will notice that the latest distributions are slightly smaller. This isn't necessarily a concern, as the TTM yield calculated with the last payout was still 19.44%.

However, for those who bought higher, the distributions are falling, as is the yield.

A purchase one year ago is yielding 17.5% and is likely to keep falling as the NAV erodes. This is apparent even when the S&P 500 is trading at new all-time highs.

This is an aspect sometimes overlooked by income investors. If SPYT drops to $10 and pays out monthly distributions of $0.16 ($2 annually), it can still advertise a 20% yield. However, for anyone holding at $20 from 2024, the distributions would be half of what they initially received, and their yield would be only 10%.

Yield vs. Return

Whether SPYT is right for your income portfolio is really a personal preference. Even though the yield looks likely to decrease over time, it will remain high and attractive. Many investors are willing to forfeit capital gains for this yield.

There are other options in this space, including very similar funds from Roundhill and Yieldmax. There is not much history to make a full comparison of returns, but what we have this year suggests that SPYT's strategy wins.

In the slightly longer term, XDTE has a slight edge, but there is very little difference.

Conclusions

SPYT provides exposure to the S&P 500 and aims for a steady 20% yield. That sounds attractive and would be fine if the ETF price stayed constant, but it is slowly falling as the NAV is eroded by ROC used to top up the distributions. Those who bought near $20 now have a 17.5% yield, even when the underlying S&P 500 is trading at new all-time highs. Over months and years, this yield could drop further, especially if the S&P 500 were to trend lower.
